content.problemStatement:
There are NNN cells (numbered 111 through NNN). Initially, each cell contains a single Chef.

You are given QQQ pairs of cell ranges [L1,R1],[L2,R2],‚Ä¶,[LQ,RQ][L_1, R_1], [L_2, R_2], \ldots, [L_Q, R_Q][L1‚Äã,R1‚Äã],[L2‚Äã,R2‚Äã],‚Ä¶,[LQ‚Äã,RQ‚Äã]. Consider a sequence of integers a1,a2,‚Ä¶,aNa_1, a_2, \ldots, a_Na1‚Äã,a2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,aN‚Äã, where 1‚â§ai‚â§N1 \le a_i \le N1‚â§ai‚Äã‚â§N for each valid iii. For each kkk from 111 to QQQ, the following process is performed:


For each iii (Lk‚â§i‚â§RkL_k \le i \le R_kLk‚Äã‚â§i‚â§Rk‚Äã), the Chef from cell iii moves to cell aia_iai‚Äã. All Chefs move at the same time.
We check if each cell contains exactly one Chef afterwards. If there is a cell which does not contain exactly one Chef, the sequence aaa is invalid and we stop.
You should find a sequence aaa which is not invalid. (Note that for such a sequence, each cell contains exactly one Chef at any point.) In addition, you are given an integer ppp with the following meaning:


If p=0p = 0p=0, you should find the lexicographically smallest sequence aaa.
If p=1p = 1p=1, you should find the lexicographically largest sequence aaa.
constraints: 0‚â§p‚â§10 \le p \le 10‚â§p‚â§1
1‚â§N,Q‚â§2‚ãÖ1051 \le N, Q \le 2 \cdot 10^51‚â§N,Q‚â§2‚ãÖ105
1‚â§Li‚â§Ri‚â§N1 \le L_i \le R_i \le N1‚â§Li‚Äã‚â§Ri‚Äã‚â§N for each valid iii
the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2‚ãÖ1052 \cdot 10^52‚ãÖ105
the sum of QQQ over all test cases does not exceed 2‚ãÖ1052 \cdot 10^52‚ãÖ105
Subtasks**Subtask #1 (15 points):** p=0p = 0p=0
**Subtask #2 (35 points):**
the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 10310^3103
the sum of QQQ over all test cases does not exceed 10310^3103
**Subtask #3 (50 points):** p=1p = 1p=1
Example Input 1`2 0
`Example Output 1`1 2 3 4 5 1 7

`Explanation**Example case 1:** Whenever a Chef should move from some cell iii, we have ai=ia_i = iai‚Äã=i, so the Chefs do not move. That means the sequence [1,2,3,4,5,1,7][1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 1, 7][1,2,3,4,5,1,7] is valid and it can be proved that it is lexicographically smallest, too.
Example Input 2`2 1
`Example Output 2`7 5 4 3 2 7 7
`Explanation**Example case 1:** The Chefs move like this:
first, 2‚Üí5,3‚Üí4,4‚Üí3,5‚Üí22 \rightarrow 5, 3 \rightarrow 4, 4 \rightarrow 3, 5 \rightarrow 22‚Üí5,3‚Üí4,4‚Üí3,5‚Üí2; then, there is still exactly one Chef in each cell
next, Chef 777 stays in his cell
finally, the Chefs in cells 333 and 444 swap places

There is no point when there are two Chefs in a single cell at the same time, so the sequence [7,5,4,3,2,7,7][7, 5, 4, 3, 2, 7, 7][7,5,4,3,2,7,7] is valid and it can be proved that it is lexicographically largest, too.
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains two space-separated integers TTT and ppp, where TTT denotes the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and QQQ.
QQQ lines follow. For each valid iii, the iii-th of these lines contains two space-separated integers LiL_iLi‚Äã and RiR_iRi‚Äã.
content.outputFormat:
For each test case, print a single line containing NNN space-separated integers a1,a2,‚Ä¶,aNa_1, a_2, \ldots, a_Na1‚Äã,a2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,aN‚Äã.


If p=0p = 0p=0, the sequence a1,a2,‚Ä¶,aNa_1, a_2, \ldots, a_Na1‚Äã,a2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,aN‚Äã should be the lexicographically smallest valid sequence.
If p=1p = 1p=1, the sequence a1,a2,‚Ä¶,aNa_1, a_2, \ldots, a_Na1‚Äã,a2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,aN‚Äã should be the lexicographically largest valid sequence.
